name: Duck Typing

laws: Suitability is decided by whether required members exist, not by declared type
Checked at use site (dynamic) or at instantiation (C++ templates)
x.encoding: Native to dynamic languages; in this set it appears via C++ templates (compile-time duck typing on member existence) and reflection-based access in C#/Java.

def: Treating a value as usable purely because it has the required members/operations, regardless of its declared type or any interface it nominally implements.
problem: Operate over anything that "fits", without forcing every supplier to declare a shared interface.
mechanism: Dynamic languages dispatch on whether the needed method exists at call time. C++ templates are compile-time duck typing: a template compiles for any type providing the used members. There is no nominal contract — only the used shape matters.
limits: Errors surface late (runtime, or deep template instantiation)
whenNot: Large teams/APIs where discoverability matters (use interfaces/concepts)
myths: "C++ templates are statically typed so not duck typing" — they are compile-time duck typing on member use
